content.assessment: This proposal's central political strategy is to use demographic decline as cover for what would otherwise be framed as an ideological reversal, which is a coherent feasibility logic but one contingent on the state successfully controlling substitution effects outside its jurisdiction. It extends an administrative tool already demonstrated to be usable for constraining selective-track expansion, suggesting institutional capacity exists, but the same director lobbying access that constrains other proposals applies here with comparable force.
opportunities: Systematic extension of the segregation-based denial precedent could build a track record and normalize administrative contraction as routine governance rather than exceptional intervention
strengths: Leverages demographic decline as depoliticizing cover, potentially reducing the perceived ideological stakes of contraction and broadening the coalition willing to accept it
Extends an administrative discretionary tool with a documented precedent of successful use, suggesting institutional capacity for enactment without new legislation
threats: Church and foundation schools outside Klebelsberg jurisdiction could absorb displaced demand, expanding their own selective tracks and neutralizing the net effect of state-sector contraction
Documented director lobbying access to top leadership constitutes a concrete channel through which this policy could be resisted or reversed
weaknesses: Contraction without admission-mechanism reform risks concentrating remaining places even more among advantaged families, undermining the equity rationale used to justify the policy
Relies on ministerial discretion, which is inherently reversible by a differently disposed minister without legislative change, unlike a legislated structural reform
